topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on the mahogany desk, an unwelcome intrusion. Inside, the results were stark: a percentage, a match, a lineage that felt utterly irrelevant. I picked up the printout, the crisp paper feeling alien under my fingertips. My jaw tightened, a muscle twitching near my temple. I stared at the genetic markers, seeing only a collection of random codes, not a story. It was a manufactured narrative, a convenient piece of paperwork designed to smooth over some messy truth. I crumpled the paper slowly, the sound a sharp, brittle noise in the quiet office. "Of course," I muttered to the empty room, the word tasting like ash. They had played their game, and I was just the discarded prop.

[story 2]

When the news finally landed, it wasn't a shock; it was a profound sense of being utterly misplaced. I stood by the window, watching the city lights blur through the rain-streaked glass. A coldness settled deep in my chest, a heavy, unmovable weight. I reread the summary of the DNA report, my eyes skimming over the data as if it were poorly written fiction. My shoulders slumped, a deliberate act of resignation. I didn't feel anger, only a deep, pervasive sense of superiority, as if this revelation simply confirmed my initial assessment: that I was inherently better suited for a different path. I turned away from the window, the movement stiff, and walked toward the door, eager to escape the suffocating implications of the findings.

[story 3]

The consultation room smelled sterile, a scent that only amplified my irritation. Dr. Evans had presented the data with an air of practiced benevolence, but I saw the underlying maneuver immediately. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, the fabric of my jacket pulling taut. My gaze never left the screen displaying the comparison charts. Every line of information seemed designed to diminish me, to recontextualize my entire existence into a footnote of someone else's history. A faint, almost imperceptible tightening around my mouth was the only outward sign of the churning resentment inside. I waited for the inevitable, the clumsy explanation that would somehow justify this inconvenient truth.
